title: Situational Leadership Model
content:
Module 2, Lesson 4: Situational Leadership Model

The Hook

A leadership approach that works brilliantly with one person on one task fails completely with another person on a different task. The experienced surgeon who confidently leads a high-stakes operation may be helpless as a new volunteer at a community fundraiser. The charismatic visionary who rallies an organisation around a new strategy may lose the room completely when the conversation turns to quarterly budget planning. The core insight of situational leadership theory is that effective leaders are not defined by a fixed style — they are defined by their ability to adapt their style to the demands of the situation.

The Concept

1. The Origins of Situational Thinking

Fiedler's Contingency Model (1967) was the first major situational theory, arguing that leadership effectiveness depends on the match between the leader's fixed style and the favourability of the situation (defined by leader-member relations, task structure, and position power). Fiedler's controversial conclusion — that it is easier to change the situation than to change the leader — undermined practical development applications. More influential for practitioners was the work of Paul Hersey and Kenneth Blanchard, whose Situational Leadership Theory (1969, later refined as the Situational Leadership® II model) provided a flexible, actionable framework that became one of the most widely used leadership development tools globally.

2. The Two Axes: Task and Relationship Behaviour

Situational Leadership builds on the Ohio State behavioural framework. Directive behaviour (task orientation) involves telling people what to do, how to do it, when to do it, and where to do it — setting goals, establishing timelines, and providing close supervision. Supportive behaviour (relationship orientation) involves listening, facilitating, encouraging, praising, and involving followers in problem-solving and decision-making. Both dimensions are rated high or low, producing four leadership styles: S1 (Directing — high directive, low supportive), S2 (Coaching — high directive, high supportive), S3 (Supporting — low directive, high supportive), S4 (Delegating — low directive, low supportive).

3. Development Level: The Key Situational Variable

The critical variable in Situational Leadership II is the follower's Development Level for a specific task, defined by the combination of competence (knowledge, skills, and experience) and commitment (motivation and confidence). Four development levels: D1 — Enthusiastic Beginner (low competence, high commitment — new to the task but keen); D2 — Disillusioned Learner (some competence, low commitment — has hit the difficulty curve); D3 — Capable but Cautious Performer (high competence, variable commitment — capable but lacks confidence or motivation); D4 — Self-Reliant Achiever (high competence, high commitment — fully capable and motivated). Crucially, development level is task-specific: the same person may be D4 in their area of expertise and D1 in a new responsibility.

4. Matching Style to Development Level

The prescriptive heart of Situational Leadership is matching leadership style to development level: D1 → S1 (Directing): give clear instructions and close supervision; the beginner needs to know exactly what to do. D2 → S2 (Coaching): maintain direction but increase dialogue and encouragement; the disillusioned learner needs both structure and support. D3 → S3 (Supporting): reduce task direction but maintain relationship focus; the capable but cautious performer needs encouragement and involvement in decisions. D4 → S4 (Delegating): give responsibility and autonomy; the self-reliant achiever is stifled by over-management. Mismatches in either direction cause problems: over-managing a D4 employee creates frustration; under-managing a D1 employee creates failure.

5. Practical Application and Limitations

Situational Leadership's practical power lies in shifting the diagnostic question from "what is my leadership style?" to "what does this person need, for this task, right now?" This requires managers to accurately diagnose development level (a skill itself), flex their style deliberately (many managers have a dominant style they default to), and hold developmental conversations that surface the follower's competence and commitment. The model's limitations include: it can oversimplify complex interactions; accurate diagnosis is difficult; and the D3 category (capability without commitment) has been challenged conceptually. Nevertheless, it remains a valuable diagnostic and communication framework.

The Illustration

Situational Leadership teaches the most important meta-skill of management: flexibility. The rigidly directive manager stifles capable performers. The uniformly supportive manager creates dependency in those who need guidance. Effective managers read the developmental needs of each team member for each task and adapt accordingly — being simultaneously more directive with one person and more delegating with another. This diagnostic agility, built on accurate observation and honest conversation, is the hallmark of situational mastery.

quiz.question: In Situational Leadership II, what two dimensions define a follower's Development Level?
Which leadership style (S2) is appropriate for a D2 follower (some competence, low commitment)?
What is the appropriate leadership style for a D4 follower (high competence, high commitment)?
Why is Development Level considered task-specific in Situational Leadership?
options: Because all tasks require the same level of competence
Because the same person may be highly competent in one task and a complete beginner in another
Because task difficulty is the only relevant factor

quiz.question: What was the key limitation of Fiedler's Contingency Model that made Situational Leadership more practical?
options: Fiedler argued it was easier to change the leader's style than the situation
Fiedler argued it was easier to change the situation than the leader's fixed style, undermining development applications
Fiedler focused only on senior management
